clean_story:
The universe’s tape measure. Light stretches across time.

The phrase cosmic ruler fits because light’s speed—299,792 kilometers per second—never changes, letting astronomers measure distances like a surveyor’s laser. Here’s how: A star 10 light-years away isn’t just far; its light took 10 years to reach us. Telescopes act like time machines. The Andromeda Galaxy’s light left 2.5 million years ago, when mastodons roamed Earth. Redshift works like a siren’s pitch drop as an ambulance speeds away—except with galaxies. The farther they are, the more their light stretches toward red. Hubble’s Law turns this stretch into a distance calculator: velocity = Hubble constant × distance. For nearby stars, parallax (Earth’s orbit shifting our viewpoint) adds precision, like triangulating a ship’s position from two lighthouses.

Three related subjects are time dilation in space, dark energy’s expansion push, and how lasers map the moon.
